content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
I Practices Commission
September 11,
1992
James R.
Sutton
Nielsen,
Merksamer,
Parrinello, Mueller and Naylor
591 Redwood Highway,
#4000
Mill Valley,
CA
94941
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—561
Dear Mr.
Sutton:
This is in response to your letter requesting reconsideration
of the advice provided in our first letter to you
(Sutton Advice
Letter,
No.
1-92—351)
regarding the applicability of the “protocol
exception” to your hypothetical situation.
Your question
concerning this interpretation of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”)
1 is general in nature.
You have not identified your firm’s
client
(the “company”),
nor does it appear from your letter that
you are authorized to request advice on behalf of the unnamed
local official.
Therefore, pursuant to Regulation 18329,
I can
give you only general information about the application of the Act
to the hypothetical situation you have presented.
2
It is your position that when a local public official asks a
company to fund and host a reception for certain individuals
designated by the official, the full value of the event should not
be construed as a gift from the company to the official.
This is
because you believe the factual situation presented in your letter
is analogous to the Disneyland ticket situation set forth in the
White Advice Letter,
No.
1—92—218,
and,
as such, the “protocol
exception” should be applied to hosting a reception as it is to
free admission tickets to California tourist attractions.
You
state that the third parties for whom the company will host the
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
4D vision
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-92-561
Page
2
reception at the behest of the local official are also visiting
dignitaries
(defined by you as foreign business or political
leaders “visiting town”),
and that apart from these two requests
and recommendations,
the company
(and not the official)
would have
sole discretion and responsibility for hosting the event.
We do not find your analysis persuasive.
The Commission
formulated the “protocol exception” in recognition of the fact
that certain public officials are frequently asked to obtain
complimentary admission tickets to tourist attractions located in
their districts for third parties,
such as visiting foreign
dignitaries or public officials,
or special groups, i.e.,
schools
or charitable organizations.
In order to preclude these officials
from being construed as the recipients of the “gift” of the
tickets in those situations,
the “protocol exception” was devised
and applied,
provided that certain other conditions were met.
3
We do not believe the factual situation you present for our
consideration,
a reception hosted by a company, is analogous to
the one contemplated by the Commission,
admission to a California
tourist attraction,
such as Disneyland in the City of Anaheim.
The only common denominator between your situation and the one
posited in the White letter appears to be the types of individuals
included in the proposed guest list.
4
This is an insufficient
basis upon which to extend the “protocol exception.”
As discussed
above, the “protocol exception” was intended to address a specific
factual situation and has limited applicability.
This strict
interpretation of the “protocol exception” to the definition of
“gift” is in keeping with the rule which provides that an
exception to the general rule must be narrowly construed.
(Estate
of Banerlee
(1978)
21 Cal.3d 527,
540.)
Furthermore, under the circumstances you present, it appears
that the local official y be receiving a “contribution,” and not
a “gift,”
from the company as we noted in footnote
3 in our prior
letter to you.
Under the Act?
a payment made “at the behest” of
an official is a contribution, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that the reception is not made for
political purposes.
(Section 82015.)
Regulation 18215 clarifies
when a payment is made for “political purposes.”
It states that
the payment is made for political purposes if the payment is
(1)
for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of
a candidate; or
(2)
received by or made at the behest of a
For example, the “protocol exception” does not apply to public
ficials or their agents who gain actual physical pos.session or
have discretion and control over the distribution of the
-
complimentary tickets to the tourist attraction.
Please note,
however, that foreign business leaders are not
necessarily “visiting dignitaries” and subject to the “protocol
exception.”


Our File No.
1-92-561
Page
3
candidate
(unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances
that the payment was received or made at the official’s behest for
personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as
an official).
Without specific facts, it is not possible to determine
whether the payment in the situation you presented, the funding of
the reception on behalf of the local official,
would be considered
a gift or a contribution to the official.
(See,
Grindle Advice
Letter,
No. A-85-198.)
The “protocol exception” does not apply to
contributions.
We trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested.
If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at
(916)
322-59Ol.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Couns
By:
Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division
SH: DS: aa
copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lOc per page.

sections.question: Whether the 'protocol exception' to the definition of 'gift' should apply to a situation where a company funds and hosts a reception for visiting dignitaries at the request of a local public official, analogous to the complimentary admission ticket situation addressed in the White Advice Letter.
sections.conclusion: We do not find your analysis persuasive. The Commission formulated the 'protocol exception' for specific factual situations involving complimentary admission to tourist attractions. The 'protocol exception' does not apply to receptions hosted by a company, as this factual situation is not analogous to tourist attraction admissions. Additionally, the payment may constitute a 'contribution' rather than a 'gift' under the Act, and the 'protocol exception' does not apply to contributions. Without specific facts, it cannot be determined whether the funding would be a gift or contribution.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the 'protocol exception' for complimentary admission tickets to tourist attractions apply to a company-hosted reception for visiting dignitaries requested by a local official?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declines to extend the 'protocol exception' to company-hosted receptions, finding the factual circumstances sufficiently distinct from tourist attraction admissions where the exception applies. The funding may constitute a contribution rather than a gift, to which the exception does not apply in any case.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter responding to a reconsideration request of a prior advice letter (1-92-351). The document clearly presents the requestor's position and the FPPC's analysis and conclusion. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers exist, but these elements are clearly identifiable in the document structure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the 'protocol exception' to the definition of 'gift' should apply to a situation where a company funds and hosts a reception for visiting dignitaries at the request of a local public official, analogous to the complimentary admission ticket situation addressed in the White Advice Letter.

CONCLUSION: We do not find your analysis persuasive. The Commission formulated the 'protocol exception' for specific factual situations involving complimentary admission to tourist attractions. The 'protocol exception' does not apply to receptions hosted by a company, as this factual situation is not analogous to tourist attraction admissions. Additionally, the payment may constitute a 'contribution' rather than a 'gift' under the Act, and the 'protocol exception' does not apply to contributions. Without specific facts, it cannot be determined whether the funding would be a gift or contribution.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to a request for reconsideration of prior informal advice regarding whether the 'protocol exception' to gift definitions applies to a company-hosted reception for visiting dignitaries requested by a local official. The FPPC concludes the exception does not apply, as it was narrowly designed for complimentary tourist attraction admission, not receptions, and the payment may constitute a contribution rather than a gift.
